text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC — URGENT UNSCHEDULED VISIT
Reason for Visit: Niraparib-related Grade 3 thrombocytopenia — CBC review and clinical evaluation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1 5382insC), currently on niraparib 200 mg daily as first-line maintenance therapy following complete response to primary debulking surgery and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. She presents urgently today for evaluation of newly detected severe thrombocytopenia identified on a scheduled CBC at week 3 of niraparib. She was contacted directly by our nurse navigator following the lab result and brought in same day.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Delacroix was diagnosed approximately 9.5 months ago with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), in the setting of a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74, the Eastern European 5382insC founder mutation). She underwent primary debulking surgery approximately 8 months ago achieving complete gross resection (R0), followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 every 21 days (paclitaxel dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 in cycles 5 and 6 due to cumulative Grade 3 sensory neuropathy). At completion of chemotherapy, she achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response — CA-125 normalized to 18 U/mL, PET-CT showed no FDG-avid disease.

Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, HRD-positive tumor profile (HRD score 56), and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, she was initiated on niraparib maintenance therapy approximately 3 weeks ago. Given her weight of 72 kg (below the 77 kg threshold per individualized starting dose [ISD] protocol derived from the PRIMA trial), she was started at 200 mg orally once daily rather than the standard 300 mg, with the explicit intent of mitigating hematologic toxicity risk. Baseline CBC prior to starting niraparib: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Platelets 189,000/uL, ANC 2,400/uL.

Today's CBC, drawn at week 3 per protocol monitoring schedule, returned with platelets 42,000/uL. The patient was called urgently. She denies any symptoms of bleeding — specifically, no gingival bleeding, no epistaxis, no petechiae or purpura noted at home, no unusual bruising, no hematuria, no melena or hematochezia, no prolonged bleeding from minor cuts. She has not taken any NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants. She reports mild fatigue over the past week but attributes this to baseline post-chemotherapy status. She has been taking niraparib consistently at bedtime as instructed and has not missed any doses. She has not had any intercurrent illnesses, infections, or viral syndromes. She denies fever, chills, or night sweats.

She took her last dose of niraparib last night (approximately 16 hours prior to this visit). She has been instructed to hold niraparib pending today's evaluation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue (stable, Grade 1), no fever, no unintentional weight loss.
Hematologic: No spontaneous bleeding, no bruising, no petechiae. No transfusion history outside of the single intraoperative pRBC transfusion during debulking surgery.
Neurologic: Residual bilateral foot and hand tingling from paclitaxel neuropathy — improving compared to the Grade 3 neuropathy at end of chemotherapy; left foot drop substantially resolved. No new neurologic symptoms.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea in the evenings, which she attributes to niraparib (taking with food has helped somewhat). No vomiting, no melena, no abdominal pain.
Dermatologic: No rash, no petechiae, no ecchymoses noted by patient.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Vaginal dryness managed with lubricants (no hormonal agents given BRCA1 status).
Other systems: Negative.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16, Temperature 98.2°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 72.1 kg. BMI: 26.4 kg/m2.
General: Alert, pleasant, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Understandably anxious about the abnormal lab result.
HEENT: Oropharynx clear. Mucous membranes moist. No gingival bleeding. No oral mucosal petechiae or blood blisters. Conjunctivae without pallor or icterus. No scleral injection.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No respiratory distress.
Abdomen: Well-healed midline laparotomy scar. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No ascites. No pelvic masses.
Extremities: No edema. No petechiae on extremities. No ecchymoses. Skin warm and well-perfused.
Neurologic: Sensation to light touch and proprioception mildly reduced in toes bilaterally (residual Grade 1 paclitaxel neuropathy, improved). No foot drop at this visit.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or ecchymoses on trunk, arms, or legs. No rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC with differential (today):
  - WBC: 5.4 x 10^3/uL
  - ANC: 1,800/uL (no neutropenia)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, stable/slight decline from 11.2 g/dL at niraparib initiation)
  - MCV: 91 fL
  - Platelets: 42,000/uL (Grade 3 thrombocytopenia; baseline at niraparib start: 189,000/uL)
Peripheral blood smear reviewed (ordered urgently):
  - No schistocytes. No platelet clumping or pseudothrombocytopenia artifact on EDTA specimen confirmed with citrate tube. No blast forms. No hypersegmented neutrophils. Platelet morphology: scattered large platelets consistent with peripheral consumption/bone marrow response pattern. No dysplastic features in granulocytic or erythroid lineages. No evidence of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA).
Comprehensive metabolic panel: BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.84 mg/dL (stable; no evidence of carboplatin nephrotoxicity), electrolytes within normal limits, LFTs within normal limits (AST 22, ALT 18, Alk Phos 74, Total Bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL). LDH normal (187 U/L — arguing against hemolysis).
CA-125: 16 U/mL (normal, stable — no biochemical evidence of disease recurrence).
Coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT): within normal limits — no coagulopathy.

---

HEMATOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE SUMMARY (obtained today)
Dr. Hendricks (Hematology) was contacted and reviewed the case. Assessment: thrombocytopenia most consistent with drug-induced myelosuppression from niraparib (PARP inhibitor-mediated on-target effect on hematopoietic progenitor cells). Pattern of decline — rapid, isolated predominant thrombocytopenia occurring at 3 weeks (classically the nadir timing for niraparib-related thrombocytopenia) without anemia disproportionate to chemotherapy history, without MAHA, and without blast forms — argues strongly against ITP, TTP/HUS, or treatment-related MDS at this time. No bone marrow biopsy indicated acutely given the clinical picture; biopsy deferred unless thrombocytopenia fails to recover on niraparib hold or worsens further. Recommend supportive management and dose modification per manufacturer guidelines.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Grade 3 Thrombocytopenia — Niraparib-related
   - Platelets nadir 42,000/uL at week 3 of niraparib 200 mg daily. Clinically asymptomatic (no bleeding, no petechiae, no transfusion-requiring hemorrhage). Peripheral smear without MAHA, without blasts, without dysplasia — pattern consistent with on-target PARP inhibitor-mediated suppression of megakaryocytic lineage in the bone marrow.
   - Niraparib HELD effective today per package insert (hold for platelets <100,000/uL). Patient was already instructed to hold last dose last night by nurse navigator.
   - CBC to be repeated in 1 week (will call patient with results and determine readiness for restart).
   - Plan to resume niraparib at one dose level reduction: 150 mg orally once daily (from 200 mg), once platelets recover to ≥100,000/uL on two consecutive checks or clear upward trajectory confirmed. This is consistent with the ISD dose modification algorithm.
   - Patient instructed STRICTLY to avoid all platelet-inhibiting medications: no aspirin, no NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, etc.), no anticoagulants. Acetaminophen acceptable for pain.
   - Fall precautions discussed given thrombocytopenia risk (minor trauma, head trauma to be avoided). Patient verbalized understanding.
   - No platelet transfusion indicated currently (asymptomatic, platelets not <20,000/uL, no active bleeding, no surgical procedures planned).
   - Bone marrow biopsy deferred; will reconsider if platelets fail to recover within 3-4 weeks of niraparib hold or if cytopenias evolve in a pattern inconsistent with drug effect.

2. Grade 1 Anemia (Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL)
   - Mild, stable decline from baseline 11.2 g/dL at niraparib initiation. Likely multifactorial — residual bone marrow suppression from completed carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy plus niraparib contribution. No symptoms of symptomatic anemia (no dyspnea on exertion, no chest pain, no orthostasis). Transfusion not indicated. Will monitor with weekly CBCs. ESA not initiated at this time given mild degree.

3. Stage IIIC High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma, BRCA1 Germline (5382insC) — Complete Remission
   - CA-125 today 16 U/mL (normal, stable). No clinical or biochemical evidence of disease recurrence.
   - No new imaging indicated at this time; surveillance CT at approximately 6-month mark from end of chemotherapy still on schedule.
   - Niraparib maintenance remains the correct and guideline-concordant strategy for this patient (germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, HRD-positive, complete response to platinum — among the highest-benefit subgroups in SOLO-1 and PRIMA data). Every effort should be made to keep her on maintenance therapy at whatever dose is tolerated, as duration of treatment correlates with outcomes in this population.

4. Residual Peripheral Neuropathy (Grade 1, improving)
   - No change in management. Physical therapy completed; patient continues home exercises. No new neuropathic symptoms.

5. Menopausal Symptom Management
   - Venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms — unchanged. Vaginal lubricants as needed. No hormonal therapy given BRCA1 status and personal oncologic history.

6. Psychosocial
   - Patient visibly anxious about the platelet result and its implications for her cancer treatment. Reassured that this is a well-recognized, expected, and manageable toxicity of niraparib — not indicative of disease recurrence, MDS, or treatment failure. Referral to oncology social work renewed. Patient verbalized understanding and appropriate reassurance.

Return to clinic in 1 week for repeat CBC. Patient provided with clear instructions on when to call urgently (any signs of bleeding — gum bleeding, blood in urine or stool, unusual bruising, or head trauma). Nurse navigator contact number reviewed. All questions answered. Patient left in stable condition.

Attending Gynecologic Oncologist: [Signature on file]
Time spent: 45 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care)
